serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of braised mixed vegetables weighs approximately 250 g, typical of a side dish serving at a Singaporean hawker centre or zi char stall.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Foods in Singapore — Braised Mixed Vegetables (F14080004)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14080004 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore food composition database with confidence 0.95. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are taken from HPB at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.6 g/100g) and trans fat (0.0 g/100g) are consistent with the vegetable-oil-braised profile and accepted as nutritionally plausible values consistent with HPB compositional data for similar cooked vegetable dishes. Cholesterol (2 mg/100g) is a trace amount consistent with possible use of oyster sauce. Vitamin C (16 mg/100g) is plausible given the capsicum and cauliflower content and consistent with HPB data for similar dishes. All secondary nutrient values are treated as supplementary estimates consistent with the HPB primary record.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — analogous estimate for non-starchy mixed vegetables
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (University of Sydney GI Database contributing authors: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific braised mixed vegetable combination (cauliflower, capsicum, carrot, bamboo shoot, green beans, black mushroom) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to non-starchy mixed vegetables using published values from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283: cauliflower GI ~15, green beans GI ~15, capsicum GI ~15, bamboo shoot GI ~15, black mushroom GI ~10–15, carrot (cooked) GI ~39. A weighted composite estimate across the six vegetables yields approximately GI 15 (low category, GI < 55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 2.61 / 100) × 2.5 = 0.98, rounded to 1.0. The input record cited GI 15 with confidence 0.562 from an embedded GI database; this is consistent with the analogous estimate and is accepted.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 149 mg/100g; high potassium-to-sodium ratio from vegetables supports blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: At 149 mg sodium per 100 g, this dish is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared with reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce; the high vegetable fibre and potassium content align with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (2.61 g/100g), high dietary fibre (2.66 g/100g), and low GI (~15) support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.61 g carbohydrate and 2.66 g dietary fibre per 100 g, this dish has a very low glycaemic load (~1.0 per 250 g serving) and is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; request minimal cornstarch thickening and reduced oyster sauce to limit added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g/100g), zero trans fat, high dietary fibre (2.66 g/100g), and antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very low saturated fat (0.6 g/100g), confirmed zero trans fat, and high fibre from cruciferous vegetables and mushrooms make this dish cardioprotective; the vitamin C from capsicum and cauliflower further supports vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vegetable profile; low saturated fat; beta-glucans from black mushroom
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The combination of cruciferous vegetables (cauliflower), beta-glucan-rich black mushrooms, antioxidant-rich capsicum and carrot, and very low saturated fat supports an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor capsicum intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Mixed Vegetables is a Singaporean Chinese side dish consisting of cauliflower, capsicum (bell pepper), carrot, bamboo shoot, green beans, and black mushroom (shiitake), braised together in a light sauce typically made with oyster sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and a small amount of oil, sometimes thickened with cornstarch. The dish is soft in texture with a savoury, mildly sweet flavour profile derived from the natural sugars of the vegetables and the umami of the oyster sauce and mushrooms. It is commonly served as a side dish at Singaporean hawker centres, zi char stalls, and Chinese restaurants, often accompanying steamed rice or noodles. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate, high-fibre dish at 38 kcal per 100 g, with 2.66 g dietary fibre, 2.61 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), and 0.8 g protein per 100 g. Sodium is moderate at 149 mg per 100 g. The dish is rich in vitamin C (16 mg/100g) from capsicum and cauliflower, and provides iron (0.78 mg/100g) and calcium (19 mg/100g). Its low glycaemic index (~15) and high fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-compatible when prepared without animal-derived sauces. It is a staple of Singaporean home cooking and hawker cuisine, known in Mandarin as 炖杂蔬 and in Malay as Sayur Campur Rebus.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore food composition database (confidence 0.95) with exact match to cr_id F14080004. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C) are nutritionally plausible and consistent with the dish profile. GI is an analogous estimate consistent with published non-starchy vegetable GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this braised mixed vegetable dish is not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data not reported in the HPB source record for this dish; an estimate of ~220 mg/100g appeared in the input health_suitability narrative but is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore not entered as a numeric value. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB source record for F14080004 and are not available in a directly applicable approved source entry for this specific dish combination; limited published data for this traditional preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed as zero per HPB compositional data; consistent with a vegetable-oil-braised dish containing no hydrogenated fat ingredients. The 'keto' dietary tag present in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been omitted from dietary_flags as the dish provides 2.61 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 6.5 g per 250 g serving, which is not definitively keto-classified without further dietary context. Cholesterol (2 mg/100g) is a trace amount consistent with possible use of oyster sauce; retained as a plausible supplementary value consistent with the HPB primary record profile. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct measurement exists for this specific dish combination. Nutrient source is the HPB Singapore food composition database; GrabFood is not used as a primary evidence source — values originally tagged to GrabFood in the input (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) have been re-evaluated as supplementary estimates consistent with the HPB primary record and are not cited as independent sources.
